Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar

# Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

![Image result for ishwar chandra vidyasagar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!CRSG!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F291bfec2-7ef1-4213-b598-849958157f1d_625x820.jpeg "Image result for ishwar chandra vidyasagar")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar, one of the icons of the Bengali renaissance. His actual surname was Bandopadhyaya, but he used his title Vidyasagar when he became a law Graduate. True to his name, he was truly an Ocean of Knowledge. There were so many facets to him, author, thinker, activist, social reformer. And above all a true humanist, one who reached out to the underprivileged, the faceless, the oppressed and downtrodden.

He established over 35 girls’ schools between 1857 and 1858, and even set up a night school and free homeopathy clinic for Santhal tribals in Karmatar, where he spent his final years.

His *Borno Porichoy* simplified the Bengali alphabet, making literacy more accessible. He’s often called the *Father of Bengali Prose*. His relentless advocacy led to the Hindu Widow Remarriage Act of 1856, despite fierce opposition from conservative groups.

He didn’t just reform society—he lived among the marginalized, choosing empathy over elitism. His humility was legendary; even Ramakrishna admired him as the “sea of wisdom”.

Born to a Brahmin family in Midnapore district, in 1820, his childhood was that of extreme poverty. He received his education in the local village paatshala, where he learnt the basic reading, writing and arithmetic skills. There was an anecdote about how he mastered the English numbers by reading on milestones. He was a favorite student of Pandit Kalikantha Chatterjee who was also his mentor of sorts too.

Along with his father he later migrated to Kolkata, where his father worked as a clerk in a metal store at Barobazar. The metal store in Barobazar, where his father worked, wasn’t just a workplace—it was the backdrop to a quiet revolution. From the cramped quarters of Bhagabat Singh’s house on Doyehata Street (now Digambar Jain Temple Road), young Ishwar studied under gas lamps and milestones, absorbing knowledge with an intensity that defied his circumstances.

Thanks toMadhusudhan Bachaspati’s intervention, Ishwar enrolled in Sanskrit College and mastered both Sanskrit and English. This dual fluency allowed him to bridge traditional Indian scholarship with modern reformist thought—a rare blend that made his writings accessible and transformative.

1839 wasn’t just the year Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar passed his Sanskrit law examination; it was the moment he earned the title that would define his legacy. “Vidyasagar”—*Ocean of Knowledge*—wasn’t a ceremonial flourish.

It was a recognition of his mastery across a spectrum of classical disciplines.

**Kavya (Poetry)**, where absorbed the lyrical and epic traditions of Sanskrit, which later shaped his elegant prose and translations. Alankara, his command over figures of speech gave his writings a persuasive and poetic edge. In Vedanta, he delved into the philosophical core of Hindu thought, grounding his reformist ideas in spiritual depth.

In Smriti his understanding of traditional law and customs enabled him to challenge orthodoxy from within. And Nyaya sharpened his reasoning, evident in his tracts on widow remarriage and polygamy, where he dismantled opposition with clarity and precision.

At just 21, Vidyasagar was appointed **Principal Lecturer in Sanskrit** at Fort William College, thanks to the support of **G.T. Marshall**, the secretary who recognized his brilliance. This role gave him exposure to British educational frameworks and deepened his understanding of administrative systems.

When he joined as **Assistant Secretary**, Vidyasagar didn’t just settle into the role—he shook the foundations of the institution. His report proposed sweeping reforms.

He advocated for the inclusion of **English and Bengali** alongside Sanskrit, making education more accessible and relevant.He pushed for the introduction of **European history, philosophy, and science**, bridging classical learning with contemporary thought.He challenged caste-based restrictions, recommending that **non-Brahmin students** be allowed to enroll.He introduced **monthly exams**, **tuition fees**, and even **Sunday as a weekly holiday**, which was revolutionary at the time.

These reforms weren’t just academic—they were ideological. Vidyasagar believed that education should empower, not exclude. His vision laid the groundwork for a more egalitarian and modern Bengal.

In 1849, Vidyasagar resigned from Sanskrit College due to serious disagreements with **Rasamoy Dutta**, the then Secretary. The core issue was that of c**aste-based admissions**. Dutta insisted on maintaining the college as a Brahmin-only preserve, while Vidyasagar championed **meritocracy and inclusivity**, believing that education should be accessible to all, regardless of caste.

After resigning, Vidyasagar briefly returned to **Fort William College** as a head clerk. But this wasn’t a retreat—it was a recalibration. His time there allowed him to continue influencing educational policy while staying true to his values.

This episode reflects his unwavering commitment to social reform—not just in theory, but in action. He didn’t just write about equality; he risked his career for it.

Vidyasagar’s return to Sanskrit College in 1849 wasn’t just a comeback; it was a resurgence of reformist fire. By **1851**, he became the **first Indian Principal** of the college, and in **1855**, he was appointed **Special Inspector of Schools** for the districts of Hooghly, Midnapore, Burdwan, and Nadia.

As Inspector, Vidyasagar wasn’t confined to ivory towers—he **traveled extensively**, visiting remote villages and inspecting schools firsthand. What he saw disturbed him deeply, many schools lacked proper buildings, furniture, or even basic supplies. Rote learning dominated, with little emphasis on critical thinking or practical knowledge.

Girls and lower-caste children were often denied access to education.He recognized the urgent need for teacher training and proposed a **normal school** to train 60 teachers annually.

In response, he established **20 model schools**, introduced **monthly exams**, and pushed for **girls’ education** with unmatched zeal. This phase laid the groundwork for his later campaigns—like the Widow Remarriage Act and the founding of Bethune School.

His role as **Special Inspector of Schools** gave him firsthand exposure to Bengal’s rural despair—where illiteracy was rampant and girls were denied even the basic right to learn. Vidyasagar saw education not as a privilege, but as a **moral imperative**.

He established **20 model schools** across districts like Hooghly, Midnapore, Burdwan, and Nadia. Between **1857 and 1858**, he founded **35 girls’ schools**, a revolutionary act in a society that viewed female literacy with suspicion.

He didn’t just fund them—he **personally visited homes**, convincing reluctant parents to send their daughters to school. He even created the **Nari-Shiksha Bandar**, a memorial fund to support women’s education.

Vidyasagar’s persuasive charm extended to **wealthy zamindars and landowners**, whom he urged to contribute land and funds for schools. He also **donated from his own salary**, often sacrificing personal comfort to keep these institutions running.

Vidyasagar didn’t just challenge customs—he **confronted power**. His advocacy for **widow remarriage**, **female education**, and **property rights** for women was seen as a direct assault on entrenched orthodoxy.

He was **threatened with violence**, even **death**, by reactionary groups like the Dharma Sabha, who feared his influence. Despite this, he **petitioned the British government**, leading to the **Hindu Widow Remarriage Act of 1856**.

He **quoted ancient texts** like the *Parashara Dharma Samhita* to prove that widow remarriage was not against Hindu dharma. He **organized public debates**, wrote tracts like *Bidhobabivah*, and **met officials like John Peter Grant**, the Lieutenant Governor of Bengal, to push for legislative change.

Vidyasagar’s empathy wasn’t abstract—it was **visceral**. He saw widows cast out, forced into prostitution, stripped of dignity. He **personally arranged widow marriages**, including one for a 10-year-old widow named Kalimati.

He **paid off debts** incurred from supporting ostracized families and widow remarriages.He was mocked, boycotted, and vilified—but he stood firm, saying, “If I die in this cause, let it be so.”

Vidyasagar’s reform wasn’t rebellion, it was reclamation. He didn’t seek to dismantle Hinduism; he sought to **restore its humane essence** by diving deep into the scriptures and surfacing truths that had long been buried beneath rigid orthodoxy.

He cited texts like the *Parashara Dharma Samhita* to argue that **widow remarriage was not against Hindu dharma**. He believed in **scriptural reinterpretation**, much like **Swami Dayananda Saraswati**, who championed the Vedas as a source of rational and ethical guidance. His approach echoed **Swami Vivekananda’s** later emphasis on spiritual upliftment through inner reform, not external rejection.

He **married his own son, Narayan Chandra Bandyopadhyay, to a widow**, defying societal backlash, showing he could walk the talk. His tireless campaigning led to the **Hindu Widow Remarriage Act**, passed on **16 July 1856** and enacted on **26 July 1856**. The law **legalized widow remarriage** across East India Company territories, marking a watershed moment in Indian social reform.

Published in **1855**, *Varna Porichoy* (also known as *Barnaparichay*) was designed to teach Bengali to children, but its impact went far beyond classrooms. Vidyasagar **standardized the Bengali alphabet**, simplifying it to **12 vowels and 40 consonants**, and introduced **punctuation**—a first in Bengali prose.

He replaced archaic, Sanskrit-heavy constructions with **short, rhythmic, and lucid sentences**, making prose accessible to the common reader. The book’s **two parts** gradually built up from letter recognition to spelling and short prose passages, laying the groundwork for modern Bengali children's literature.

Gurudev Tagore called him the **Father of Modern Bengali Prose**, acknowledging how Vidyasagar’s style paved the way for literary giants like **Bankim Chandra Chatterjee** and **Tekchand Thakur**. His prose was **secular, moralistic, and rhythmically balanced**, emphasizing character formation over religious instruction—a bold stance for the time. From 1855 to 1891, *Varna Porichoy* saw **152 editions**, with over **33 lakh copies printed**, a testament to its enduring popularity.

Vidyasagar wasn’t just a reformer or educator, he was a **cultural bridge**, bringing the wisdom of Sanskrit epics into the Bengali vernacular with clarity and elegance.

**Betal Panchavinsati in 1847, a** Bengali retelling of the *Vetala Panchavimshati*, originally part of the *Kathasaritsagara*, that comprised **25 tales** of moral dilemmas and riddles posed by the ghostly Betal to King Vikramaditya. His version is known for its **lucid prose**, making complex Sanskrit storytelling accessible to Bengali readers.

He translated key portions of Mahabharata in 1860, especially the *Upakramanika*, offering ethical and philosophical insights. Adapted Kalidasa’s *Abhijnanashakuntalam*, blending romance and dharma, wrote Seetar Vanvas in 1860, inspired by *Uttara Ramayana* and *Bhavabhuti’s Uttararamacharita*, this work explores Sita’s exile with emotional depth. And Banglar Itihas in 1848, a historical narrative based on Marshman’s *History of Bengal*, tailored for Bengali readers to understand their own heritage.

Having grown up in poverty, Vidyasagar knew what it meant, and this made him a great humanist and philanthropist too. As a student surviving on meager scholarships, Vidyasagar would **cook kheer** and distribute it to the poor on the streets of Kolkata.

He **donated most of his salary** to support the poor in his native village, Birsingha.He **established schools and healthcare centers**, including a **free homeopathy clinic** and **night schools for adults** in Karmatar, where he spent his final years. His home, **Nandan Kanan**, became a sanctuary for tribal communities, offering education and medical care.

Locals called him **“Dayar Sagar”**—Ocean of Kindness. He cremated unclaimed bodies, admitted students from so-called lower castes, and **nursed the sick back to health**.His philanthropy was **quiet, consistent, and deeply personal**—not driven by fame, but by a moral compass that never wavered.

As **Principal of Sanskrit College**, Vidyasagar broke tradition by **admitting students from lower castes**, challenging the Brahminical monopoly on Sanskrit education. He **dined with so-called untouchables**, a radical act in 19th-century Bengal that shattered social taboos and sent a powerful message of equality.

He believed that **knowledge belonged to all**, not just the privileged few—a stance that earned him both admiration and fierce opposition.

During **cholera outbreaks in Calcutta**, Vidyasagar would **personally visit patients**, often those abandoned due to caste stigma.He administered **homeopathic treatments**, kept detailed medical diaries, and even walked miles to reach remote villages.

He was also proud of his Indian roots, and immensely self-respecting.He never tolerated insults based on race. When Vidyasagar visited **Mr. Kerr**, the Principal of Hindu College, and saw him sitting with his feet on the table—a gesture dripping with colonial condescension—he took note, but didn’t react immediately.Later, when **Mr. Kerr came to Vidyasagar’s home**, Vidyasagar **mirrored the gesture**, placing his own feet on the table.

He wore **home-spun dhoti-kurta**, refused to adopt Western attire, and was once **denied entry to a British club** for not dressing “appropriately.” In response, he **never sought entry again**, choosing self-respect over validation.

Sadly disillusioned by **pettiness within his own family**, Vidyasagar chose solitude over bitterness. He spent his last **18 years in Karmatar**, now in Jharkhand, living among the **Santhal tribals**, where he was revered like a sage.

His home, **Nandan Kanan**, became a haven of compassion—he ran a **free homeopathy clinic**, started a **girls’ school**, and taught **adult night classes**. The Santhals called him **“Doctor Babu”**, not for his degrees, but for his healing presence.

Vidyasagar passed away on **29 July 1891**, at the age of 70.His death marked the end of an era, but his legacy continues to ripple through **education, literature, and social justice**.

The best tribute came from Gurudev Tagore

*“One wonders how God, in the process of producing forty million Bengalis, produced a man!”*
